concerns.analysis: The image manifest indicates that all extracted images in the HTML have corrupted base64 data ('valid': false, with error 'No module named \'PIL\''). Because the image data is corrupted, none of the meaningful images from the source PDF (such as the cover photo on page 1, the NC State University logo, and Figures 1 through 14) will render in the browser.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: Meaningful images including the cover photo and NC State University logo on page 1, and maps/charts in Figures 1-14.
concerns.output_quote: Image manifest shows '"valid": false' and '"error": "No module named 'PIL'"' for all 17 extracted images.

concerns.description: Table caption coded as header row: 1 table(s) have a single spanning first row that is likely a caption/title
concerns.analysis: caption_as_header=1. Examples: p6: "Roanoke Rapids". First row spans all columns — should be a <caption> element, not a header row.

concerns.description: Header/footer elements retained in output (35): 2023 Roanoke Rapids Vegetation Survey Report; 1; 2023 Roanoke Rapids Vegetation Survey Report
concerns.analysis: header_footer_count=35. Header/footer text from extraction JSON is present in the rendered HTML — the renderer failed to strip it. This is a structural accessibility issue.

The source document features underlines for the text 'Prepared By:' and 'Submitted To:' on the cover page. The extraction outputs these terms using markdown italics (*Prepared By:*) rather than HTML underline tags, resulting in a loss of the specific underline emphasis.

concerns.description: Displaced hyperlinks: 1 link(s) extracted as standalone elements instead of inline text
concerns.analysis: standalone_links=1, examples=['https://www.dominionenergy.com/projects-and-facilities/hydro']. Links should be inline within paragraphs, not separate blocks.

concerns.description: Table header markup issues: 1 table(s) with 3+ rows have no <th> header cells
concerns.analysis: data_in_th_time=0, no_th_tables=1. Minor semantic markup issues: tables lack <th> elements or use time values as headers. These affect screen reader context but are less severe than data misrepresentation.
